prompt: who is APRA AMCOS
reference: APRA AMCOS is the trading name of Australasian Performing Right Association Limited and Australasian Mechanical Copyright Owners Society Limited. It pays royalties to music creators when their music is played or copied locally and overseas. Over 124,000 songwriters, composers, and publishers appoint APRA AMCOS to look after their rights. APRA has an administrative partnership with AMCOS but does not own or control it.

prompt: how does APRA AMCOS distribute royalties to songwriters
reference: APRA AMCOS collects licence fees from broadcasters and digital service providers and distributes them as royalties. For unidentified songs under blanket licences, distribution points are carried forward until copyright owners are identified and paid in the next distribution. For transactional licences including streaming, earnings are held in the APRA Suspense Account until identified. For non-member writers, cash is held in an APRA Royalty Adjustment Account until they join.

prompt: what is the difference between APRA and AMCOS
reference: APRA stands for Australasian Performing Right Association Limited and manages public performance rights of songwriters, composers, and publishers. AMCOS stands for Australasian Mechanical Copyright Owners Society Limited and manages mechanical reproduction rights. Together they form APRA AMCOS, operating under a single trading name. They are two distinct organisations that operate in alliance with different distribution policies.

prompt: how do I become a member of APRA AMCOS
reference: To join APRA AMCOS, complete the membership enquiry form on the website. APRA AMCOS conducts an initial assessment; if successful, you receive a full application form to complete and return. Applications are sent to the APRA Board for approval, which occurs each quarter. You can apply to APRA, AMCOS, or both. APRA offers Publisher Full Membership and Associate Membership. AMCOS also provides two membership types for rights administrators. Membership is free for songwriters and composers.

prompt: explain how music licensing works in Australia
reference: Music licensing in Australia is governed by the Copyright Act 1968, managed primarily by APRA AMCOS. Two main rights exist: performing rights for public performance and communication, and mechanical rights for reproduction. Businesses need a public performance licence when playing music in cafes, restaurants, or broadcasts. Mechanical licences cover reproduction on physical or digital formats.

prompt: what types of licences does APRA AMCOS offer
reference: APRA AMCOS offers several licence types: Dramatic Context licences for stage productions; Community Radio licences covering broadcast, reproduction, and online use for AM/FM stations; a Limited Digital Music Service Agreement for streaming or download releases; licences for places of worship; production music licences for synchronisation in advertisements, films, TV programs, and websites; theatrical licences; communication licences; and PlayStation Network licences.

prompt: what happens if a business plays music without an APRA licence
reference: Playing music without an APRA licence may infringe copyright and result in legal action. For events live streamed on an unlicensed platform, the licence fee is 11% of all revenue generated, with a minimum fee of $82.50 per event. If the event is free, the fee is 11% of event expenditure. Businesses must comply with platform terms and conditions. Streaming pre-recorded audio-visual recordings may require additional synchronisation licences.

prompt: how are streaming royalties calculated by APRA AMCOS
reference: APRA AMCOS calculates streaming royalties using licensing agreements and usage data. For fitness businesses, the AMCOS licence fee is distributed using Spotify and Apple Music Premium subscriber data. Points are calculated per song, and 38% of revenue from fitness licence fees is divided by total points to determine a rate per point, which is multiplied by each song's points to calculate the royalty. Streaming data is limited to works with 1,000 or more streams on Spotify or Apple Music premium.

prompt: compare APRA AMCOS with other collecting societies internationally
reference: APRA AMCOS represents the rights of 110 affiliated performing and mechanical societies worldwide and partners with more than 90 performing rights organisations globally. In the USA, APRA AMCOS has reciprocal agreements with ASCAP, BMI, and SESAC. Unlike most countries with a single PRO, Australia has two entities — APRA for performing rights and AMCOS for mechanical rights — operating under one trading name.

prompt: what is the OneMusic Australia licence
reference: OneMusic Australia is a joint initiative between APRA AMCOS and PPCA, launched in 2019. It provides a single public performance licence covering both musical works administered by APRA AMCOS and sound recordings administered by PPCA. This eliminates the need for separate licences, simplifying compliance for hotels, pubs, clubs, and other businesses.

prompt: what are the APRA AMCOS APRA Awards
reference: The APRA Music Awards are an annual event honouring Australian songwriters, their songs, and publishers. Eighteen awards are given across three categories: twelve based on APRA's statistical analysis of most performed works in genres including Alternative, Country, Dance, Hip Hop/Rap, Pop, Rock, R&B/Soul, and Most Performed Australian Work Overseas; one peer-voted award by APRA members; and five determined by the APRA Board of Directors, including the Distinguished Services award for outstand...

prompt: how does APRA AMCOS support emerging songwriters
reference: APRA AMCOS supports emerging songwriters through Professional Development Awards; in 2023 twelve recipients each received a $10,000 cash prize. Members connect with industry peers at networking events, with staff advising on royalties and career development. APRA AMCOS collaborates with the Country Music Association of Australia to host sessions covering song registration, copyright, and career pathways.

prompt: what is an ISWC and how does APRA AMCOS use it
reference: An ISWC (International Standard Work Code) is a unique, permanent, internationally recognised reference number for identifying musical works. ISWCs are centrally assigned by CISAC, the International Confederation of Societies of Authors and Composers. APRA AMCOS uses ISWCs to match reported usage to works in their system, ensuring accurate royalty distribution.

prompt: how do I report a live performance to APRA AMCOS
reference: Submit Performance Reports through the APRA AMCOS App or Writer Portal. Log in and submit setlists from your live performance with details of all songs performed so APRA AMCOS can determine which works are eligible for royalty distribution. Performance Reports must be submitted within one year from the date of the performance. Only APRA writer members can submit reports on their own or their band's behalf.

prompt: what is a performing right
reference: A performing right is the right to perform literary or musical works publicly. APRA, the Australasian Performing Right Association, manages performing rights on behalf of its members. Performing rights cover public performance and communication of musical works, including radio or television broadcasts, playing music in public venues, and online streaming.

prompt: what is a mechanical right
reference: A mechanical right covers the reproduction of musical works in physical or digital formats for sale, streaming, or download. AMCOS, the Australasian Mechanical Copyright Owners Society, administers mechanical rights in Australia and New Zealand. AMCOS licences the reproduction of music and pays royalties to copyright owners who have unpublished music or manage their own mechanical reproduction rights. Mechanical rights relate to the underlying musical work as distinct from the sound recording.

prompt: how does APRA AMCOS handle music used in films and television
reference: APRA AMCOS collects licence fees from Australian and New Zealand commercial free-to-air television networks for broadcast of music in television shows, films, promos, and advertisements, covering Network 7, 9, and 10 and their regional and digital channels. Collected fees are distributed to composers, publishers, and songwriters. Television station data is used to allocate royalties and maximise payments.

prompt: what is the APRA AMCOS distribution schedule
reference: APRA AMCOS distributes royalties according to a schedule that varies by licence category and usage type. For international performances, payment timing depends on the affiliated overseas society's distribution frequency and can take up to two years after the performance date. APRA AMCOS holds royalties for unidentified works in suspense accounts and pays them once the copyright owner is identified.

prompt: can overseas songwriters join APRA AMCOS
reference: Overseas songwriters can join APRA AMCOS if they meet eligibility criteria. To join AMCOS, you must be a copyright owner, not already belong to an overseas mechanical rights organisation, and have at least one unpublished work commercially reproduced on digital services, as a physical product, or in a production music recording. To join APRA, you must write your own songs and have them performed live, broadcast, or communicated to the public.

prompt: what is the role of the APRA AMCOS board of directors
reference: The APRA Board is elected by full APRA members, ensuring members' voices are represented at the highest level. Directors bring expertise from the music and rights management sectors, guiding management in strategy, governance, and advocacy. The board approves key policies including the Modern Slavery Act 2018 statement, reviewed annually. The board determines the Distinguished Services award for outstanding services to Australian and New Zealand music.

prompt: how does APRA AMCOS collect royalties from digital platforms
reference: APRA AMCOS licences Digital Service Providers such as Spotify, Apple Music, Amazon Music, Vevo, and SoundCloud operating in Australia and New Zealand, who pay fees directly to APRA AMCOS. Fees cover both APRA performing rights and AMCOS mechanical reproduction rights for underlying musical works, not sound recordings. APRA AMCOS distributes collected royalties to members including songwriters, composers, publishers, and international affiliates based on DSP usage data.

prompt: what is the difference between a songwriter and a music publisher
reference: A songwriter creates the musical and lyrical content of a song; a music publisher manages the business aspects including rights administration, royalty collection, and strategic development. Publishers nurture songwriters, manage rights attached to songs, and set up collaborations and co-writes locally and internationally across genres. APRA AMCOS represents both songwriters and music publishers with separate membership types.
